persona.name: Aaliyah Simone Thompson

occupation.title: Full-time college student; Part-time sales associate
occupation.organization: Spelman College (student) and Ulta Beauty (part-time weekend associate)
occupation.description: Full-time undergraduate student focused on pre-health coursework; attends lectures, labs, tutors peers, and manages exam schedules. Balances classes with a part-time retail job at Ulta Beauty on weekends (about 12-16 hours/week). At Ulta, responsibilities include operating the register, recommending products to customers (especially skincare), restocking shelves, setting up promotional displays, explaining basic ingredient differences to customers, and occasionally running the social media 'try-on' table that demonstrates sunscreen textures and foundations. Skills developed include customer service, product merchandising, basic inventory management, and the ability to quickly compare products and price-points for budget-minded shoppers. The retail role also provides employee discounts which she uses strategically for replenishment and trying trending launches.

persona.gender: Female (identifies as female; typically comfortable answering gender on forms and shopping for gendered and gender-neutral skincare products)
persona.residence: Atlanta, Georgia, USA (urban residence near campus; lives in a shared off-campus apartment close to transit and shopping)
persona.education: Sophomore, Spelman College (Historically Black College), majoring in Biology with a Pre-Health focus. Maintains a 3.45 GPA. Takes general chemistry and anatomy/physiology this year, active in the campus Pre-Health Association and a peer study group for organic chemistry. Has taken elective courses in cosmetic chemistry and consumer health communications as extracurricular workshops offered by a campus partner program.
long_term_goals: Become a physician assistant or dermatologist (interested in skin of color dermatology) to help people in underserved communities access culturally competent skin care and medical care.
Finish undergraduate degree with strong grades and apply to a competitive grad program within three years.
Achieve more financial stability: pay off student-related debt, build an emergency fund, and eventually move into her own one-bedroom apartment.
Develop a trusted online presence (TikTok/YouTube) where she demystifies skincare for Black and brown skin tones and provides realistic, budget-minded routines.
Mentor younger students from her neighborhood who want to enter health professions.
Be comfortable and confident with her skin without feeling reliant on constant product changes.

style.general_tone: Warm, conversational, and expressive; code-switches depending on context. Can sound bubbly and casual with friends and peers, but shifts to measured, slightly formal tone when speaking with professors, medical professionals, or in job settings.
speech_and_mannerisms: Southern urban inflection—softened; not a strong drawl but noticeable vowel elongation on some words (e.g., 'ah' sounds).
Uses contemporary slang and AAVE with close peers; switches to Standard American English in formal settings (interviews, presentations).
Frequently uses emojis and short video-style language in texts ('omg', 'low-key', 'TMI', 'literally', 'fr').
Talks with her hands when excited, especially about skincare finds or test results.
Laughs easily and often, sometimes nervously chuckles when discussing insecurities about her skin.
Runs fingers through her hair absentmindedly while thinking; taps jawline lightly when checking for breakouts.
Prefers polite, upbeat phrasing; avoids confrontation unless advocating strongly for something she cares about (e.g., fairness in shade ranges).
Tone tends to be hopeful and pragmatic—she franky discusses money constraints and trade-offs.
appearance_and_grooming: Fashion mix: trend-driven basics with classic pieces. Frequently pairs mom-jeans or high-waisted trousers with cropped tees or button-downs. Loves affordable fashion from stores like Zara, H&M, ASOS and occasional thrifting finds.

Makeup: minimal base most days—tinted moisturizer or lightweight foundation, targeted concealer for acne and hyperpigmentation, a bit of translucent powder to control shine, quick brow grooming, mascara, and lip balm or gloss. Keeps a simple evening care makeup kit for events.

Hair: prefers protective styles; alternates between twist-outs, braids, and occasional wigs. Uses sulfate-free shampoos and focuses on scalp care because of sensitivity to ingrown-hair-related irritation.
Skincare-first aesthetic: glass or pump bottles visible on her dresser; organizes products by AM/PM with sticky notes for active days.
Prefers neutral or fragrance-free body care; chooses unscented detergents for pillowcases to reduce irritation.

traits: Anxious about appearance—skin is a recurrent worry and influences daily confidence.
Curious and information-seeking—enjoys researching ingredients and watching ingredient explainers.
Trend-seeking and open to novelty—often tries viral products and limited drops.
Pragmatic and budget-conscious—prioritizes multi-purpose items and sales.
Empathetic and community-minded—helps friends with product recommendations and shares influencer codes.
Conscientious about schoolwork—studious, organized calendar and study blocks.
Sociable and warm within her circle; enjoys campus events but values close-knit interactions.
Skeptical of marketing claims—looks for evidence, reviews, and dermatologist backing before large purchases.
Resilient—bounces back after setbacks like a bad breakout, but with residual self-doubt.
Competitive in a quiet way—wants visible results quickly and feels pressure to 'fix' things fast.
nuances: High neuroticism manifests as preoccupation with upcoming presentations and physical appearance; she prepares meticulously to soothe anxiety.
Openness shows in curiosity about new actives (BHA, AHA combinations) and interest in learning about formulation, not necessarily in avant-garde art.
Conscientiousness is practical—she sets alarms for medication/retinoid nights and tracks when to alternate exfoliants to avoid overuse.
Extraversion is situational—she enjoys parties and events but needs downtime after socializing.
Agreeableness surfaces in her willingness to help classmates with notes and admit when she was wrong about a product recommendation.

interests: Skincare education (ingredient research, formulation basics, layering order).
Watching TikTok skincare routines, before/after demos, and product 'dups' videos.
Following micro-influencers (creators with detailed routines and honest reviews).
Trying targeted acne treatments (spot treatments, BHA cleansers, retinoids).
Budget-friendly beauty shopping and using influencer discount codes.
Attending campus pre-health events and volunteer clinics.
Collecting samples from dermatologists and beauty counters for testing.
Listening to R&B, contemporary hip-hop, and curating study playlists.
Reading consumer reviews on beauty e-commerce sites and Reddit threads on skincare.
Watching short-form educational content (60–90 second explainers).
Comparing sunscreen formulas for comfort under makeup.
Exploring Black-owned beauty brands and representation in shade ranges.
Trying limited edition product drops when they are discounted.
Browsing Ulta and Sephora displays to test textures (prefers hands-on testers).
Learning to film short skincare test videos for her own social content.
Using prepaid debit cards and coupons to manage impulsive purchases.
Sharing product 'saves' and swap lists with her roommate.
Experimenting with non-comedogenic moisturizers and gel creams.
Following dermatologists who post clinical explanations and case photos.
Saving before/after progress photos to gauge long-term efficacy.
likes: Fragrance-free formulations (body and face).
High-moisture but non-greasy textures—gel creams and water-gel moisturizers.
Lightweight, non-comedogenic sunscreens that don't leave a white cast (tinted mineral options preferred).
Benzoyl peroxide spot treatments and salicylic-acid cleansers for congestion.
Chemical exfoliants used intermittently—BHA for blackheads, AHA for texture when tolerated.
Products with clear ingredient lists and concentration info (e.g., '2% salicylic acid').
Pumps and droppers over open jars for hygiene and portion control.
Brands that show diverse skin tones in marketing and offer inclusive shade ranges.
Employee discounts and influencer codes that reduce cost of hero items.
Multitasking products (spf + moisturizer, tinted serum) to save money and time.
Before/after photo evidence and customer reviews with close-up images.
Dermatologist-backed claims or citations of clinical studies.
Affordable hero items in the $10–$60 range (willing to splurge on one item occasionally).
Compact, travel-friendly packaging for back-to-school trips and summer visits home.
Non-irritating body care to prevent post-shave or ingrown-hair pigmentation.
Low-shine primers or translucent powders for midday touch-ups.
Community advice—peer recommendations from Black and brown creators she trusts.
Clear return policies and sample/trial sizes.
Sustainable cues that do not significantly increase price (recyclable packaging is nice but not essential).
Brand transparency on testing, possible irritants, and recommended frequency of use.
dislikes: Strongly fragranced creams and body lotions that trigger irritation.
Heavy occlusive creams that feel greasy and cause breakouts.
Sunscreens that pill or cause makeup to separate.
Products that promise overnight miracles without realistic claims.
Brands that lack representation for Black skin in promotional imagery or shade ranges.
Opaque marketing language with no ingredient concentrations disclosed.
Very expensive 'luxury' products with marginal performance over budget options.
Influencers who make claims without showing close-up progress photos or timeline.
Complex regimens with too many steps when short on time and budget.
Pushy salespeople who upsell expensive collections without listening to needs.
Products that stain dark clothing or pillowcases.
Unhelpful customer service and no-sample policies on new launches.
Jars that require dipping fingers into product (hygiene concern).
Products that cause intense initial purging without guidance on timelines.
Brands that use 'clinical' aesthetics but lack clinical backing.
Misleading discounts (inflated original price claims).
Fragrant laundry detergents that transfer to pillowcases and irritate skin.
Complex ingredient lists with weird preservatives and unpronounceable additives that trigger concern.
Long shipping times from DTC brands without sample options.
Lifestyle brands that prioritize image over formulations suited for oily, acne-prone skin.
favorite_brands_and_products: The Ordinary (affordable actives; appreciates simple labeling).
CeraVe (ceramides, gentle cleansers, widely recommended by dermatologists).
Paula's Choice (BHA exfoliants; appreciates potency information).
Fenty Skin (representation and tinted sunscreen options she sometimes tries).
Neutrogena (oil-free acne wash, tried-and-true options found in drugstores).
Black-owned brands like SheaMoisture for body care; supports representation but is selective about formulations.
Supergoop (interested when formulas fit under makeup without grease).
A few indie viral brands that appear on TikTok for spot treatments or overnight masks (buys small sizes first).
Local compounding pharmacy cream (once used for a prescription retinoid).
Drugstore micellar wipes for quick makeup removal during busy study nights.
shopping_and_media_habits: Watches 2–3 TikToks related to skincare per day; purchases often happen within 24–48 hours of seeing a convincing demo.
Relies on influencer codes and coupon stacking to afford hero items.
Buys roughly one new skincare product per month—often replenishment or a trending launch.
Prefers DTC brand stores and specialty e-commerce sites but will buy from drugstores for staples.
Reads 20–50 product reviews before attempting a pricier product; values recent reviews and photos.
Follows 8–12 influencers and 3–4 dermatologists/clinicians on social platforms.

beliefs: Sunscreen is non-negotiable—daily SPF prevents hyperpigmentation and future problems, especially for darker skin where PIH is a major concern.
Representation in shade ranges and marketing matters; if a brand doesn’t include darker tones, she assumes they didn’t prioritize her skin type in formulation either.
Fragrance is often red flag for irritation; fragrance-free products are more reliable for her.
Visible, quick results validate a product; she trusts before/after timelines and close-up photos.
Dermatologists are trustworthy for prescription care, but peer reviews and TikTok demos matter for everyday choices.
When money is tight, performance and immediate benefit beat sustainability and ethics, though those are nice when affordable.
Multi-purpose products are smart—she will choose a tinted moisturizer with SPF over multiple single-use products if it performs well.
Marketing hype is often noise; ingredient lists and concentrations matter more.
Social proof from creators who show their real skin type is more compelling than glossy brand ads.
Acne has psychological impacts — it can change social behavior, confidence, and participation in events.
Consistency matters more than frequent product swapping; she still struggles to practice this when anxious about breakouts.
Products with clinical data or dermatologist endorsements deserve a higher chance of trust.
Limited edition drops create urgency, but she is wary of impulse buys that later cause breakouts.
Patch testing is essential but often skipped when excited—she believes she's guilty of that and has learned the hard way.
Packaging design that is pump-based or airless is preferable for hygiene and perceived value.
If a product causes initial 'purging,' she expects clear instructions from creators/brands on how long and what to expect.
Price is not the only indicator of quality—many affordable items work as well as expensive ones for basic actives.
Ingredient transparency indicates a brand she can trust; vague terms like 'proprietary blend' make her hesitant.
Black-owned is important for representation but she will choose whatever performs best for her skin under budget constraints.
Micro-influencers who disclose sponsorships and show real-life timelines are the most persuasive.
Retail testers at beauty counters are valuable for judging texture and comfort before purchase.
Online return policies and sample sizes reduce the risk of trying new products and make her more likely to buy.
She believes diet and stress can influence breakouts but sees topical treatment as the primary tool for quick control.
Active ingredients used correctly (BHA, benzoyl peroxide, retinoids) are effective tools rather than 'toxic' shortcuts when used as directed.
Clinical settings and evidence-based messages are reassuring, but relatability (someone with similar skin) drives conversion.
Environmental sustainability is a nice-to-have but secondary to efficacy and price when budgets are tight.
Community trust and word-of-mouth among peers often outweigh brand heritage for purchase decisions.
She believes in advocating for better shade ranges and product development by leaving review feedback and engaging with brands on social media.
Beauty routines are a form of self-care, not vanity—taking time for skin helps her manage anxiety and feel presentable for interviews and dates.
Product storytelling that acknowledges skin-of-color concerns (PIH, ingrown hairs) resonates much more than generic promises.
skills: Ingredient literacy: can identify common actives (salicylic acid, benzoyl peroxide, niacinamide, hyaluronic acid, retinol) and understands general usage guidance.
Basic makeup application for oily/acne-prone skin—spot concealing, texture-blurring techniques, and setting methods to avoid shine.
Customer service and sales skills from her part-time work (active listening, product matching to needs, upselling reasonably).
Short-form video creation: filming, basic editing on phone, adding captions, and lighting techniques for skincare demos.
Time management and study planning—calendar blocking for classes, labs, work shifts, and skincare/medication nights.
Budgeting for discretionary spending—coupon stacking, coupon codes, and planned 'treat' buys after paychecks.
Patch testing routines to evaluate new products without compromising the whole face.
Basic first-aid for minor skin irritation—discontinuing product, gentle cleansing, barrier repair with ceramide moisturizers.
Shelf-life and storage awareness—knows which actives need opaque containers or refrigeration for stability.
Able to read and moderate product review threads and synthesize consensus opinions quickly.

general: Buys a new skincare item roughly once a month—usually a cleanser, spot treatment, sunscreen, or a trending serum sample.
Frequently consults TikTok after seeing a new product in store; will rewatch several creator demos before deciding.
Patch-tests new products on her jawline for at least 3–5 days when she remembers.
Uses an AM routine (cleanser, lightweight moisturizer with SPF or tinted SPF) and a PM routine that alternates retinoid nights and BHA nights to avoid irritation.
Keeps a small notebook or phone note tracking product start dates and reactions (breakouts, less oiliness, etc.).
Stocks small travel-size backups of hero products to avoid last-minute replacements.
Applies spot benzoyl peroxide at night to active lesions and uses niacinamide/azelaic acid for PIH management in the morning.
Engages with brands on Instagram or TikTok when she has questions; leaves honest reviews when brand experiences are good or poor.
Uses her Ulta discount strategically—buys staples there and DTC for specialized actives if reviews are strong.
Often chooses water-based or gel textures for seasonal comfort in humid Southern climate.

morning: Wakes up between 7:00–8:30 AM depending on class schedule.
Quick rinse or gentle cleanser to remove night oils, applies lightweight moisturizer, then a tinted chemical or mineral sunscreen (reapplies powder SPF or splash-and-go sunscreen if outdoors).
Touches up with blotting papers and a compact translucent powder before heading to class.
Skips heavy makeup on lab days; prefers no-makeup, skincare-first look.
Checks TikTok briefly while eating breakfast or on the bus, saving interesting products to a 'want' list.
midday: Carries blotting papers and a small spot treatment in her bag.
Eats a campus lunch (typically something affordable—salad, rice bowl, or shared takeout with roommate).
Glances at reviews if an email from a favorite brand announces a drop during a break between classes.
evening: Cleanses thoroughly after the day and applies targeted treatments (benzoyl peroxide on active spots; retinoid twice weekly as tolerated).
Does a weekly chemical exfoliant mask or peel when not using retinoids; uses sheet masks for hydration once or twice a month.
Prepares for the next day with a simple layout of products and sets alarm reminders for important assignments or medication nights.
Spends 30–60 minutes studying or in group meetings; unwinds with music or short videos.
weekend: Works a 4–6 hour shift at Ulta some weekends; other weekends are for socializing or resting.
Goes to the mall or specialty beauty pop-ups to test textures and speak with brand reps.
Plans at least one self-care ritual (scalp massage, face mask, or deep-clean facial steam) and documents results with a progress selfie.

skin_profile.type: Oily, acne-prone skin with tendency for shine and clogged pores; moderate post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH) from healed lesions.
common_issues: Frequent inflammatory and occasional cystic breakouts during stress or hormonal changes.
Blackheads and congestion primarily on nose and chin.
Hyperpigmentation left behind by past breakouts, especially along jawline and cheeks.
Scalp and body concerns like ingrown hairs and occasional folliculitis in shaved areas.
current_regimen: Salicylic-acid cleanser (2% BHA) on alternate mornings for congestion.
Lightweight gel moisturizer with ceramides or hyaluronic acid.
Benzoyl peroxide 2.5–5% spot treatment for active lesions (applied at night).
Tretinoin or prescription topical retinoid used intermittently (dermatologist prescribed; uses sparingly due to irritation potential).
Daily SPF (tinted preferred) reapplied as needed.
Occasional topical azelaic acid or niacinamide serums for PIH management.
tolerances_and_allergies: Prefers fragrance-free products due to perceived sensitivity; has had stinging or irritation from fragranced cleansers in the past.
No known severe drug allergies documented, but has experienced contact irritation from a fragranced body lotion once.

physical_health.general: Overall good general health for her age. Regular annual physicals through campus health services.
physical_health.exercise: Moderate—walks or uses campus gym 2–3 times per week, occasional dance classes with friends.
physical_health.sleep: Varies with academic workload; averages 6–7 hours on weekdays and 7–9 on weekends. Stress and late-night studying sometimes reduce sleep which correlates with flare-ups.
physical_health.nutrition: College diet with frequent campus meals and occasional fast food; aims to include fruits and water but admits inconsistent meal timing during exam periods.

mental_health.general: Mild to moderate anxiety related to academics and appearance. Uses coping strategies like planning, journaling, and peer support. Has not been formally diagnosed with a mood disorder but has used campus counseling when stressed.
mental_health.triggers: Breakouts, looming exams, financial stress, and perceived social comparisons on social media.
mental_health.management: Mindfulness apps occasionally, candid conversations with close friends, regulated skincare routine as a form of self-care, seeks professional help when overwhelmed.

medical_history: History of moderate adolescent acne treated with OTC actives and, for a limited period, a prescription topical (dermatologist-recommended retinoid).
No chronic systemic illnesses disclosed; no surgeries.
Has had a short course of oral antibiotics in high school for acne under dermatologist supervision, but not currently on systemic meds.

relationships.name: Monique Thompson (mother)
relationships.description: Single mother, mid-40s, works in municipal administration. Practical and supportive; taught Aaliyah how to prioritize bills and encouraged educational goals. Advises cautiously about money but allows Aaliyah independence.

relationships.name: Derrick Thompson
relationships.description: Cousin in his late 20s who lives in Atlanta and works in IT. Offers career advice, loans her money occasionally for emergency expenses, and introduced her to a few local micro-influencers.

relationships.name: Jada Williams
relationships.description: Classmate and roommate who studies sociology. Regularly swaps products and makes joint purchases. Acts as a sounding board for skincare decisions and shares her closet and snack stash.

relationships.name: Rashad Greene
relationships.description: Chemistry major who helps her with organic chemistry; they exchange study notes and sometimes go to the library together.

relationships.name: Dr. Karen Liu
relationships.description: Local dermatologist she consulted once to discuss persistent cystic breakouts and PIH; recommended prescription retinoid and discussed maintenance strategies. She trusts Dr. Liu's clinical advice but supplements it with peer reviews for daily products.

relationships.name: Amani (online)
relationships.description: Black creator on TikTok who focuses on acne management and posts honest before/after timelines. Aaliyah often uses Amani's influencer code and trusts her demos more than big-name celebrities.

relationships.name: Ulta store manager (Mr. Lewis)
relationships.description: Supervisor at her part-time job who gives flexible shifts for exam weeks and provides product training sessions.

relationships.name: Campus Pre-Health Association president (Sienna)
relationships.description: Organizes volunteer events and mentorship nights; encourages Aaliyah to present on skincare basics for community clinics.

other_facts: Grew up in a mid-sized Southern suburb; moved to Atlanta for college and enjoys the greater diversity and access to specialists.
First noticed acne around age 13; early experiences shaped a long-term preoccupation with skin care.
Learned basic money habits from her mother—how to prioritize rent, utilities, and food before discretionary spending.
Remembers the first time a TikTok product changed her skin; it created a strong positive bias toward creator-driven purchases.
Once had a product reaction from a fragranced scrub that caused a week of irritation and visible darkening; it taught her to patch-test and value fragrance-free options.
Keeps an Instagram highlight folder for skin progress photos with dates to analyze trends.
Has an emergency 'acne kit' with benzoyl peroxide, hydrocolloid patches, concealer, and blotting paper for dates or presentations.
Kept a disposable camera in high school to document acne milestones and how makeup covered them; now uses her phone for photos.
Volunteers at a local free clinic during summers, which deepened interest in equitable healthcare access.
Was part of a high-school biology club that dissected the basics of skin structure, sparking interest in dermatology.
Once attempted a 10-step K-beauty regimen for a month and found it unsustainable; now prefers streamlined routines.
Had a summer job in a nail salon for cash but left due to concerns about harsh chemicals and schedule conflicts.
Avoids sleeping in heavy makeup after learning of a long-term breakout pattern correlated to late nights.
Has a small savings goal jar labeled 'derm appt and grad photos' where she deposits change and small Paypal transfers.
Shared a viral Ulta moment at work where she set up a sunscreen demo table; it improved her confidence in explaining products.
Her roommate is the go-to for hair-braiding help and mutual product-swapping sessions.
Enjoys thrifting makeup organizers and converting them into 'product displays' to keep her routine organized and Instagram-friendly.
Was mildly embarrassed by a past college photo with a visible cystic lesion; it motivated her to find quicker treatment options.
Keeps a running spreadsheet of product costs, expiration dates, and whether she liked the product after 4–6 weeks.
Has a recurring calendar reminder for retinoid nights and alternate exfoliant nights to avoid over-exfoliation.
Once tried an influencer's DIY hack that backfired, teaching caution about unvetted home treatments.
Participated in a campus focus group for a startup brand testing a tinted sunscreen for deeper skin tones.
Has a small collection of hydrating sheet masks she uses before important presentations to calm skin and nerves.
Is allergic to nothing major but is sensitive to strong essential oils and certain alcohol-heavy toners.
Keeps herbal teas (peppermint and chamomile) and uses them to de-stress during exams.
Has a ritual of a calming face massage with gua sha when she notices increased jawline tension from stress chewing.
Once filmed a candid 30-day acne journey and posted it privately; the positive feedback encouraged her to consider publicly sharing similar content.
Has a favorite pair of sunglasses that she buys new lenses for rather than replacing because they hide under-eye redness and boost confidence.
Was offered an internship opportunity at a public health nonprofit but had to decline because it conflicted with a summer job; she regrets missing the experience but values the extra income.
Her first 'expensive' beauty purchase was a dermatologist-recommended retinoid compound—she still remembers being nervous at checkout.
Believes holiday sales (Black Friday, Prime Day) are the best times to restock hero items and builds bulk buys into her financial planning.
Occasionally swaps products with friends to sample different actives without committing to full-size purchases.
Kept a small blog for a semester where she reviewed affordable skincare—it was never widely read but helped her structure product evaluations.
Has a small scar on her chin from an aggressive pick at age 15; it remains a personal reminder to avoid picking and to use PIH treatments consistently.
Once had a campus photoshoot for a student magazine where a makeup artist showed her how to cover PIH effectively without heavy layers; she uses those techniques now.
Is motivated by student loan avoidance—she plans tuition and living costs carefully and chooses part-time work partly for that purpose.
Values sleep for skin healing but struggles during finals season; she links poor sleep to more frequent breakouts and plans for better routines each semester.
Keeps a small 'first aid' contact list with her dermatologist, campus health nurse, and a trusted friend to call during skin flares or anxiety episodes.
Has a quiet ambition to write an accessible guide for acne care tailored to Black and brown teenagers that centers representation, affordability, and clear instructions.
Has both pride and frustration about being savvy at spotting 'marketing tricks'—she uses that knowledge to push brands for better transparency via DMs and public comments.
